title: Signals
subtitle: The future of Angular reactivity — fine-grained, predictable, fast
description: Master Angular Signals — the modern reactive primitive that replaces Zone.js change detection. Learn signal(), computed(), effect(), signal inputs, and how Signals interop with RxJS.
keyPoints: A Signal is a reactive value wrapper — reading it tracks a dependency, writing it schedules an update
signal(value) creates a writable signal; read it by calling it as a function: count()
computed(() => expr) creates a derived read-only signal that recalculates only when dependencies change
effect(() => sideEffect) runs a function whenever any signal it reads changes
input() and input.required() are signal-based component inputs — cleaner than @Input()
Signals enable zoneless Angular — faster, more predictable change detection without Zone.js
toSignal() converts an Observable to a Signal; toObservable() does the reverse

sections.title: Why Signals? The Problem With Zone.js
sections.content:
Before Signals, Angular used Zone.js to detect when data changed and the UI needed to update. Zone.js works by monkey-patching every async operation in the browser (setTimeout, fetch, Promises, event listeners) and telling Angular to re-check the entire component tree after each one.

This approach has real problems:
• Every async operation triggers change detection — even ones that don't affect the UI
• Angular has to check the entire component tree top-to-bottom on each cycle
• Zone.js adds ~100kB to every Angular app
• The behaviour is implicit and hard to reason about
• Debugging 'why did this re-render?' is difficult

Signals solve all of these problems. A Signal tracks exactly which parts of the UI depend on it. When a Signal changes, only those specific parts update — no full tree traversal, no Zone.js, no guessing.

sections.caption: Zone.js checks every component on every async event. Signals update only the exact DOM nodes that depend on the changed signal — orders of magnitude more efficient.

sections.title: signal() — Creating Reactive Values
sections.content:
A Signal is a value container with two superpowers:
1. Reading it in a reactive context (computed, effect, template) registers a dependency
2. Writing to it notifies all dependents to update

You create a writable signal with signal(initialValue). The generic type is inferred automatically.

Reading a signal: call it like a function — count(). This is intentional — calling it makes the dependency tracking explicit in code.

Writing to a signal — three methods:
• .set(value)           — replace the value completely
• .update(fn)           — derive new value from current: count.update(n => n + 1)
• .mutate(fn)           — mutate in place (for objects/arrays — use sparingly)

sections.title: signal() — All Write Methods
sections.explanation: Always use update() with a new object/array reference rather than mutating in place. Signals use strict equality (===) to detect changes — mutating an object without creating a new reference means the signal value IS the same object, so no update fires.

sections.content: Never mutate an object or array in a signal directly. This is a common mistake: tags.update(t => { t.push('new'); return t; }) — this returns the SAME array reference, so Angular sees no change and the UI does not update. Always return a NEW array or object: tags.update(t => [...t, 'new']).

sections.title: computed() — Derived Read-Only Signals
sections.content:
computed() creates a signal whose value is derived from one or more other signals. It is lazy and memoised:

• Lazy — the computation only runs when the computed signal is first read
• Memoised — if the dependencies haven't changed, it returns the cached value without recomputing
• Automatic — Angular tracks which signals are read inside the computation function and re-runs it only when one of them changes
• Read-only — you cannot call .set() or .update() on a computed signal

computed() can depend on other computed signals, creating a reactive dependency graph that Angular maintains automatically.

sections.title: computed() — Derived Signals
sections.explanation: When updateQty() is called, items() changes → subtotal() recalculates → tax() and total() recalculate → isFreeShipping() and amountToFreeShipping() recalculate. Angular does this automatically by tracking the dependency graph.

sections.caption: Angular builds this dependency graph automatically by tracking which signals are read inside each computed(). You never manually declare dependencies.

sections.title: effect() — Reacting to Signal Changes
sections.content:
effect() runs a function whenever any signal it reads changes. Unlike computed(), it is used for side-effects — things that don't produce a value but DO something in response to a change.

Good uses for effect():
• Syncing to localStorage when state changes
• Logging for debugging
• Updating a third-party (non-Angular) library (charts, maps)
• Triggering animations
• Sending analytics events

Important rules:
• effect() must be called inside an injection context (constructor, class field, or inject() call)
• By default, you cannot write to a signal inside an effect — use { allowSignalWrites: true } if necessary, but prefer computing derived state with computed() instead
• effects are cleaned up automatically when the component is destroyed

sections.title: effect() — Side Effects
sections.explanation: Each effect() independently tracks its own dependencies. The theme effect only re-runs when theme() changes. The fontSize effect only re-runs when fontSize() changes. They are completely independent.

sections.content: If you find yourself writing effect(() => { derivedValue.set(a() + b()); }), stop — that is what computed() is for. Use effect() only for true side-effects (DOM manipulation, localStorage, logging, third-party libs). Prefer computed() for any value you want to derive from other signals.

sections.title: Signal Inputs — input() and input.required()
sections.content:
Angular 17.1 introduced signal-based component inputs as a replacement for @Input() decorator.

• input<T>()           — optional input with an optional default
• input<T>(defaultValue) — optional input with a specific default
• input.required<T>()  — required input — Angular throws a compile-time error if not provided

Signal inputs are read-only computed signals. You cannot set them from inside the component — they are controlled by the parent. But they compose naturally with computed() and effect(), unlike @Input().

With withComponentInputBinding() in the router config, route parameters are also automatically bound to signal inputs by name.

sections.title: Signal Inputs — input() and input.required()
sections.explanation: computed() inside a component can read input() signals — when the parent changes [product], computed signals like stars() automatically recalculate. This is impossible with the old @Input() without implementing ngOnChanges manually.

sections.title: output() — Signal-Based Event Emitter
sections.content:
output() is the signal-era replacement for @Output() + EventEmitter. It creates a typed event emitter with a simpler API.

• output<T>() — creates an output that emits values of type T
• output() — creates an output with no value (like a void event)
• call .emit(value) to fire the event

Differences from @Output() + EventEmitter:
• No need to import EventEmitter
• No @Output() decorator — just call output()
• Cannot be subscribed to as an Observable from outside (which was an anti-pattern anyway)
• Clearly communicates intent — it is an event emitter, not a signal
• Works the same way in parent templates: (myOutput)="handler($event)"

sections.title: output() vs Old @Output()
sections.explanation: output() is cleaner — no imports of Output or EventEmitter, no decorator syntax. The behaviour in the parent template is identical: (changed)="handler($event)" works with both styles.

sections.title: Signals and RxJS — toSignal() and toObservable()
sections.content:
Signals and RxJS Observables are complementary — you can freely convert between them using two utility functions from @angular/core/rxjs-interop.

toSignal(observable) — converts an Observable to a Signal:
• The signal updates whenever the Observable emits
• Automatically unsubscribes when the calling component is destroyed
• You can provide an initialValue for the signal before the first emission

toObservable(signal) — converts a Signal to an Observable:
• Emits whenever the signal changes
• Useful for feeding a signal into an RxJS operator chain

Typical patterns:
• HTTP responses → toSignal() to use in templates without async pipe
• Route params (Observables from ActivatedRoute) → toSignal() for Signal-based components
• Search signal → toObservable() → debounceTime → switchMap → HTTP → toSignal()

sections.title: toSignal() and toObservable()
sections.explanation: toSignal() handles subscription and cleanup automatically. No need for takeUntilDestroyed() or async pipe. The combination of toObservable() + RxJS operators + toSignal() is the ideal bridge between the two worlds.

sections.title: asReadonly() — Encapsulating Signal State
sections.content:
When exposing signals from a service or parent component, use .asReadonly() to prevent consumers from calling .set() or .update() directly.

This enforces encapsulation — only the service or component that owns the signal can write to it. Consumers can only read the value. All mutations must go through the service's public API methods.

This pattern is especially important in services that manage shared state across multiple components.

sections.title: Signal Encapsulation Pattern
sections.explanation: asReadonly() converts WritableSignal<T> to Signal<T> — Signal has no .set() or .update() methods. TypeScript enforces this at compile time. Components that try to write will get a type error immediately.

sections.content: Signals are the foundation of Angular's forthcoming Zoneless mode — apps without Zone.js at all. When all state is managed through Signals, Angular knows exactly which components need to update without polling the entire tree. You can already try zoneless Angular today by adding provideExperimentalZonelessChangeDetection() to your app config.

interviewQuestions.q: What is an Angular Signal and how is it different from a regular TypeScript property?
interviewQuestions.a: A Signal is a reactive value container — calling signal(value) wraps a value so that reads are tracked and writes are broadcast. When you read a signal inside a computed(), effect(), or template, Angular registers a dependency. When you write to the signal with .set() or .update(), Angular automatically updates only the dependents of that signal. A regular TypeScript property has none of this — Angular has no way to know when it changes and must rely on Zone.js to trigger a full tree check.

interviewQuestions.q: What are the three ways to write to a signal and when do you use each?
interviewQuestions.a: .set(newValue) — replaces the value completely, best for setting a completely new value regardless of the current one. .update(fn) — derives a new value from the current one using a function: count.update(n => n + 1). Best for incrementing, toggling, or transforming the current value. .mutate(fn) — mutates an object or array in place without creating a new reference (deprecated in newer Angular — prefer .update() with spread syntax instead to avoid equality check issues).

interviewQuestions.q: What is the difference between computed() and effect()?
interviewQuestions.a: computed() creates a new read-only signal whose value is derived from other signals. It is lazy (only recomputes when read), memoised (caches the last result), and produces a value. Use it when you need a derived value. effect() runs a function as a side-effect when any signal it reads changes. It produces no value — it does something (syncs to localStorage, updates a third-party library, logs). Use computed() for derived state; use effect() for side-effects only.

interviewQuestions.q: Why should you not mutate objects or arrays directly inside a signal's update() function?
interviewQuestions.a: Signals use strict reference equality (===) to detect changes. If you mutate the existing object and return the same reference, Angular sees that the signal value IS the same object (same reference), concludes nothing changed, and skips the update — the UI does not re-render. Always return a NEW object or array: this.items.update(list => [...list, newItem]) creates a new array reference, which Angular correctly detects as a change.

interviewQuestions.q: What is the difference between input() and @Input()? Why prefer input()?
interviewQuestions.a: Both pass data from parent to child. @Input() is the old decorator and requires importing Input; the value is a plain TypeScript property. input() is the modern signal-based API (Angular 17.1+); the value is a Signal, so you read it with parentheses: product(). The key advantages of input(): it composes with computed() and effect() naturally (so derived values auto-update when inputs change), input.required<T>() provides compile-time enforcement, and it works without Zone.js in zoneless mode.

interviewQuestions.q: Where can you call effect() and why?
interviewQuestions.a: effect() must be called inside an injection context: inside a class constructor, as a class field initialiser, or inside an inject() factory. It cannot be called in a method that runs after construction (like ngOnInit). This is because effect() needs to register a cleanup function with the component's or service's lifecycle — Angular automatically runs this cleanup when the component is destroyed, preventing memory leaks. If you need to create an effect outside an injection context, pass an injector explicitly: effect(() => { ... }, { injector: this.injector }).

interviewQuestions.q: What does asReadonly() do and why is it important for service design?
interviewQuestions.a: asReadonly() converts a WritableSignal<T> to a read-only Signal<T> — the returned signal has no .set() or .update() methods. This enforces encapsulation at the TypeScript type level: components that inject a service can read the signal but cannot bypass the service's API to mutate state directly. This is the same pattern as making class fields private — it ensures all state changes go through the service's controlled methods, keeping the state predictable and auditable.

interviewQuestions.q: What is toSignal() and when would you use it?
interviewQuestions.a: toSignal() (from @angular/core/rxjs-interop) converts an RxJS Observable into a Signal. You provide an initialValue for the signal before the first emission. It automatically subscribes when called and unsubscribes when the component is destroyed — no takeUntilDestroyed() or async pipe needed. Common uses: converting HttpClient.get() responses to signals for use in templates, converting route params from ActivatedRoute to signals, and as the end of a complex RxJS operator chain (debounce + switchMap for search-as-you-type).

interviewQuestions.q: How do Signals enable zoneless Angular?
interviewQuestions.a: Zone.js tracks all async operations and triggers full change detection tree traversals after each one. With Signals, Angular knows exactly which components depend on which signals. When a signal changes, Angular only re-renders the specific DOM nodes that read that signal — no full tree traversal, no Zone.js needed. You can enable experimental zoneless mode today with provideExperimentalZonelessChangeDetection() in app.config.ts. This reduces bundle size by ~100kB and makes change detection faster and more predictable.

interviewQuestions.q: What is the ideal pattern for combining Signals with an HTTP search (search-as-you-type)?
interviewQuestions.a: Declare a query = signal('') for the search term. Convert it to an Observable with toObservable(query). Apply RxJS operators: debounceTime(300) to wait for the user to stop typing, distinctUntilChanged() to skip if the query hasn't changed, switchMap() to make the HTTP call and cancel any previous in-flight request. Convert the final Observable back to a signal with toSignal(obs$, { initialValue: [] }). The result is a signal the template can read directly — no subscribe(), no async pipe, automatic cleanup.
